title: Size-Limited Room Temperature Single-Photon Emission from Sidewall-Treated Fractional Dimension InGaN Quantum Dots: Determined by Density-of-States-Corrected Ultrafast Carrier Dynamics and Improved Signal-to-Noise Ratio
abstract: Room-temperature single-photon emission (SPE) resulting from a biexciton-exciton cascaded decay is demonstrated for the first time from chemically and photoelectrochemically etched site-controlled In0.14Ga0.86N quantum dots (QDs) embedded in vertical GaN nanowires. Diameter-dependent biexciton-exciton dynamics are analysed to determine the eligibility of QD as a single-photon emitter. The signal-to-noise ratio degrades with increasing QD diameter. Background noise photons pose a bottleneck to achieving SPE. This is also explained from a carrier dynamics perspective. Surface recombination contributes to inhomogeneous broadening at QD diameters larger than 35 nm. Below 35 nm, density-of-states-corrected Auger gradually becomes the principal biexciton-decay route with further reduction in QD diameter, thereby quenching the possibility of thermal broadening and setting a threshold for SPE. Below 9 nm, the Auger recombination rate becomes manyfold of other decay rates, causing multi-photon suppression via single Auger decay to form an exciton. Surface recombination probability of this exciton is minimized while biexciton state filling probability is maximized by reducing sidewall surface states through wet-treatment. These improve biexciton state preparation and enhance the single-photon purity of the exciton towards the exciton Bohr radius (3 nm) regime. Far away from this regime, higher-order autocorrelations to characterize quantum emission involving multi-photon events are discussed. This study establishes a generalized physical framework for predetermining SPE probability as a function of QD surface and geometry down to the exciton Bohr radius regime, with practical implementations. This work shows the pathway to design and develop next-generation semiconductor QDs for high-purity room-temperature SPE.
summary: This work demonstrates how to achieve high-purity single-photon emission from InGaN quantum dots at room temperature by controlling their diameter. By reducing the QD size, the researchers suppressed surface-related noise and leveraged Auger recombination to favor single-photon purity. This establishes a design pathway for robust semiconductor-based quantum light sources.
main_problem: Achieving high-purity, room-temperature single-photon emission (SPE) from semiconductor quantum dots by identifying the limiting effects of QD size and surface properties.
main_result: Demonstrated that reducing InGaN QD diameter below 31 nm suppresses surface recombination and utilizes Auger-driven decay to enable high-purity SPE at room temperature.
method: The study combines experimental fabrication (dry and wet etching) and characterization (micro-PL, HBT autocorrelation) with theoretical modeling (COMSOL/Silvaco simulations and DOS-corrected Auger recombination models).
model_or_system: Site-controlled In0.14Ga0.86N quantum dots embedded in vertical GaN nanowires, with diameters ranging from 8 nm to 36 nm, analyzed via a carrier decay model involving radiative, Auger, and surface recombination channels.
key_observables: Second-order autocorrelation g2(0), signal-to-noise ratio (r), background correction factor (rho), and spectral linewidths.
important_parameters: QD diameter (8-36 nm), exciton Bohr radius (~3 nm), and the single-photon limited diameter (31 nm).
main_assumptions: The Auger recombination rate requires a density-of-states correction to remain physically realistic in the low-dimensional regime.
figures_summary: Schematics of the heterostructure, SEM images of etched samples, and micro-PL spectra showing exciton and biexciton peaks with varying linewidths.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of room-temperature SPE, describes the fabrication and characterization of etched InGaN QDs, presents a theoretical framework for carrier dynamics and size-dependent decay, validates the model against experimental autocorrelation data, and concludes with implications for next-generation QD design.
why_interesting: The paper provides a generalized physical framework for controlling photon statistics (g2(0)) via geometric engineering and carrier dynamics, which is fundamental for developing scalable, high-purity single-photon sources for quantum networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
